g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore the Basic Performance Equation, the fundamental framework for understanding computer speed and efficiency.
g.text: Basic Performance Equation in Computer Architecture

g.data-narration: We ask: How fast does a processor execute a program? The answer involves more than just clock speed—it requires careful analysis.
g.text: "How fast does a given processor execute a program?"
Performance is not just about clock speed (3 GHz vs 4 GHz)

g.data-narration: CPU execution time depends on three factors: Instruction Count, Cycles Per Instruction, and Clock Cycle Time. Let's explore each.
The Basic Performance Equation calculates CPU time as the product of instruction count, cycles per instruction, and cycle time.
g.text: or equivalently: CPU Time = (IC × CPI) / Clock Rate

g.data-narration: Instruction Count (IC) is the total number of instructions executed. It depends on software—the algorithm, programmer, and instruction set architecture.
Cycles Per Instruction (CPI) is the average clock cycles per instruction. It's influenced by hardware efficiency and microarchitecture design.
g.text: CPI is a weighted average (different instructions

g.data-narration: Clock Cycle Time (T) is the duration of one cycle. Its inverse is Clock Rate, measured in Hertz. This depends on hardware technology.
Visualizing the equation: IC determines total workload, CPI determines how efficiently each instruction processes, and T determines the base timing.
g.text: Reducing any component reduces total CPU Time (improves performance)

g.data-narration: Let's compare two CPUs running the same program with 1,000,000 instructions. CPU-A has higher clock rate, CPU-B has lower CPI.
g.text: Question: Which CPU executes the program faster?

g.data-narration: Calculating CPU-A: With 4 GHz clock and CPI 1.2, the execution time equals one million cycles divided by four billion cycles per second.
Calculating CPU-B: With 3 GHz clock and CPI 0.9, the result is identical at 0.3 milliseconds, showing performance balance between factors.
The critical insight: CPU performance cannot be judged by clock rate alone. All three factors interact, and trade-offs between them determine actual speed.
In summary: Improve IC through better algorithms, reduce CPI with efficient pipelines, and decrease cycle time with faster hardware technology.
The Basic Performance Equation is the foundation for all computer architecture analysis and design trade-offs. Remember: CPU Time = IC × CPI × T.
g.text: CPU Time = (IC × CPI × T) — The Foundation of Computer Performance Analysis
